main_sections.title: Animals, Birds, and Dr. Dolittle
main_sections.prose_summary: This extract is adapted from Hugh Lofting's classic novel about Doctor John Dolittle, a physician in the English town of Puddleby who gradually abandons human medicine to become a celebrated animal doctor. The story opens with a chance conversation: the Cat's-food-Man, visiting Dolittle with a stomach-ache, suggests that the doctor's exceptional understanding of animals would make him a far better veterinarian than a people's doctor. The parrot Polynesia, who has been quietly listening from the windowsill, seizes on the idea and persuades Dolittle to make the switch, urging him to stop wasting his talents on ungrateful human patients. Dolittle is hesitant at first — he notes that there are already plenty of animal doctors — but Polynesia's next revelation changes everything: animals have their own languages, and she offers to teach him. An enchanting afternoon follows, with Polynesia giving Dolittle the Birds' A.B.C. while rain falls outside, and then demonstrating that even the dog Jip communicates through body language — twitching his nose to ask why Dolittle hasn't noticed the rain has stopped. Once Dolittle masters animal languages, his reputation spreads rapidly. A plough horse with failing eyesight becomes a memorable patient: the horse explains that the vet over the hill has been treating him for weeks with useless pills, whereas what he actually needs is a pair of green spectacles to shield his eyes while ploughing. Dolittle obliges, the horse's sight is restored, and soon farm animals across the Puddleby countryside are seen wearing glasses. Word spreads among animals of every kind — harvest mice, badgers, bats, and creatures from foreign lands — and Dolittle's garden is perpetually crowded with patients. He installs separate labelled doors for different species and even a tiny tunnel into the cellar for the mice. In a few years, John Dolittle, M.D., is famous across the world, happy in his work, and celebrated as the only doctor who truly understands what animals need. The story is narrated with a warm, wry humour: the horse's complaint that 'anybody thinks he can doctor animals — just because the animals don't complain' encapsulates the gentle satirical point that empathy and communication are the foundations of good medicine.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Do you have a pet or a domestic animal? If yes, why? If not, why not? How do people usually spend time with a pet or domestic animal? Share your thoughts with your classmates and the teacher.

You must have read stories where animals and birds talk. If you had a chance to communicate with an animal or a bird, who would you like to speak to and why? Share your thoughts with your classmates and the teacher.

If we closely observe the body language of animals and birds, we can understand what they want to tell us. Work in pairs and study the pictures given below. Find out what emotions these animals and birds are trying to convey. Share your observations with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Study pictures 1–4 of animals and birds and identify the emotion each is conveying.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (Part I). Complete the following sentence by selecting a suitable reason. The Cat's-food-Man suggests to Doctor Dolittle that he should become an animal doctor because he believes that Dolittle ______.

question_bank.stem: The Cat's-food-Man suggests to Doctor Dolittle that he should become an animal doctor because he believes that Dolittle ______.
options: earns less fee by treating human patients
enjoys the company of animals as they speak less
dislikes spending time with human patients
knows more about animals than the local veterinarians

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks by choosing the suitable option given in the brackets. Doctor Dolittle reacts to Polynesia's information about animal languages with both excitement and ____________ (certainty/curiosity). He rushes to ____________ (write/memorise) the bird words she provides and is interested in learning more.

question_bank.stem: Doctor Dolittle reacts to Polynesia's information about animal languages with both excitement and ____________ (certainty/curiosity).
He rushes to ____________ (write/memorise) the bird words she provides and is interested in learning more.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think Doctor Dolittle would be famous as an animal doctor? If yes, why? If not, why not?

question_bank.stem: Do you think Doctor Dolittle would be famous as an animal doctor? If yes, why? If not, why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (Part II). What became a common sight in Puddleby after Doctor Dolittle started treating animals?

question_bank.stem: What became a common sight in Puddleby after Doctor Dolittle started treating animals?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: What challenges do you think Doctor Dolittle might face if more and more animals come to him for help? How might he overcome these challenges to ensure that he provides good treatment?

question_bank.stem: What challenges do you think Doctor Dolittle might face if more and more animals come to him for help? How might he overcome these challenges to ensure that he provides good treatment?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Arrange the following events in correct order according to the story. Two have been done for you. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Arrange the following events in correct order (events 1–10 given; items (i) and (ii) pre-filled).
question_bank.table:
Event|Order
Doctor Dolittle decides to stop being a doctor for people and becomes a doctor for animals.|
Animals from near and far come to Doctor Dolittle for help, crowding his garden.|
A man with a stomach-ache visits Doctor Dolittle and suggests he should treat animals instead of people.|(i)
Polynesia teaches Doctor Dolittle animal languages, and he starts understanding and communicating with animals.|
A plough horse with vision problems visits Doctor Dolittle, who prescribes green spectacles.|
The parrot, Polynesia, encourages Doctor Dolittle to become an animal doctor, revealing that animals can talk.|(ii)
Doctor Dolittle installs special doors for different animals to enter his house for treatment.|
Doctor Dolittle's ability to communicate with animals helps him diagnose and treat their illnesses effectively.|
Doctor Dolittle gains fame among animals worldwide, and they come to him from various places for his help.|
Word spreads, and people start bringing their sick pets and farm animals to Doctor Dolittle for treatment.|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect. Read the extracts given below and answer the questions that follow. Extract 1: 'If I say, Polly wants a cracker, you understand me. But hear this: Ka-ka oi-ee, fee-fee?' … 'What would have been the good?' said Polynesia, dusting some cracker crumbs off her left wing. 'You wouldn't have understood me if I had.'

question_bank.stem: In the line, 'Good Gracious!' cried the Doctor, the expression 'Good Gracious' can be replaced by ______!
Why had Polynesia never used bird language with the Doctor earlier?
Identify whether the following statement is true or false: Polynesia had been munching on a food item while talking to Doctor Dolittle.
How did Doctor Dolittle feel according to the extract?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read extract 2: 'I would like a pair like yours,' said the horse — 'only green. They'll keep the Sun out of my eyes while I'm ploughing the field.' … 'the trouble is that anybody thinks he can doctor animals — just because the animals don't complain. As a matter of fact, it takes a much cleverer man to be a really good animal doctor than it does to be a good people's doctor.'

question_bank.stem: Choose a word from the extract to complete the analogy: digging: flower bed :: ______: field
Choose the correct word to complete the sentence. Doctor Dolittle's attitude in the extract is ______.
Based on the extract, we can conclude that doctors take animals for granted because ______.
Do you agree with the concluding sentence of the extract? Why/why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions. (1) How can we say that Polynesia was a good trainer of animal language? (2) Explain how Doctor Dolittle gave equal and individual attention to each animal who came to him for treatment. (3) How does Doctor Dolittle's readiness to follow the parrot's advice highlight the importance of listening to different viewpoints? (4) Which qualities of Doctor Dolittle made him famous among the animals? Explain with evidence from the text. (5) What changes do you think would happen in the world if people suddenly gained the ability like Doctor Dolittle, to understand and communicate with animals?

listening.transcript_text: Greetings and attention everyone! We are excited to announce the opening of Dr. John Dolittle's new clinic in the town of Woodstock. The clinic is located at 12, Rose Lane. It will be open Monday to Friday from 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Special timings for birds are on Saturdays from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m. Dr. Dolittle is renowned for his ability to communicate with animals and shall ensure that they receive the best care possible. All animals are welcome, from horses and cows to pets and wild animals. For the best veterinary care, visit Dr. John Dolittle's new clinic in Woodstock. Thank you.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to an announcement. As you listen, state whether the following statement is true or false.

question_bank.stem: Dr. Dolittle's new clinic presents an offer of free medicines.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to the announcement again. As you listen, complete the details given below with one or two exact words you hear.

question_bank.stem: The new clinic is located in the town of ______.
The address is 12, ______ Lane.
The clinic is open Monday to Friday from ______ a.m. to 5 p.m.
Special timings for ______ are on Saturdays from 11 a.m. to 2 p.m.
Dr. Dolittle will ensure that animals get the ______.
All animals — horses, cows, ______, wild animals, and others are welcome.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Conversation between a doctor and a patient. Read the information about the problems 1–4. Use the cue cards provided to guide your conversation. Take turns to play parts of doctor and patient. Problem 1: Cough and cold — Solution: Ginger and honey mix; Additional Advice: Drink warm fluids and rest. Problem 2: Dry lips — Solution: Apply ghee; Additional Advice: Stay hydrated and avoid wetting your lips. Problem 3: Sore throat — Solution: Gargle with warm salt water; Additional Advice: Drink herbal teas and avoid cold drinks. Problem 4: Fatigue — Solution: Ensure adequate sleep and a balanced diet; Additional Advice: Take short walks to increase energy levels.

question_bank.stem: Using the cue cards (Greet patient / Enquire about problem / Ask about symptoms / Make a diagnosis and give solution / Provide additional advice / Share details of next appointment), role-play a doctor–patient conversation for each of the four problems.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A notice is a means of formal communication for a particular group of persons. Write a notice in about 50 words about the Annual Health Check-up for students of Grades 6–8. Remind students to collect their health check-up cards from the class teacher. Include all other necessary details about date, time, and venue. Complete the draft of the notice by using the words and phrases given in the box: inform, queries, health card, health check-up, assembly hall, Grades 6–8, please contact, Thursday and Friday.

question_bank.stem: Complete the draft notice for the Annual Health Check-up by filling in blanks using words from the box (inform, queries, health card, health check-up, assembly hall, Grades 6–8, please contact, Thursday and Friday).
Write your own notice of about 50 words for the Annual Health Check-up for Grades 6–8, including heading, date, time, venue, and contact details.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the text: Cat's-food-Man, bird language, flowerpots. Are all the words that are combined to form another word written the same way? Compound words are formed by combining two words of different parts of speech — hyphenated (Cat's-food-Man), open compound words (bird language) and closed compound words (flowerpots). Complete the following table correctly by choosing words from the box given below: sailor song, teatime, check-in, plough horse, harvest mice, windowsill, well-bred.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Underline the names of animals that are hidden in the sentences given below (clues given in brackets). 1. Dr. Dolittle was eating oatmeal for his breakfast. (a farm animal) 2. Old ladies began to bring him their pets. (an insect) 3. The author sees the connection between humans and animals. (a farm animal) 4. The three baby dinosaurs are from ice age. (field animal) 5. The patients who came late didn't know where to go. (ship of the desert).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following sentence from the text: 'But that stupid man over the hill never even looked at my eyes.' The highlighted words ('never', 'even') spell and read the same backwards — they are palindromes. Read the sentences below and identify the palindrome words and sentences: (1) Step on no pets. (2) Naman saw a race car at the show. (3) Nitin speaks in Malayalam. (4) The engineer used a rotator to check the surface level. (5) The pilot relied on the radar to navigate safely. (6) Eva can I see bees in a cave? Now, create your own palindrome and share it.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Underline the verbs and identify the tense form in the following sentences from the text: (1) He is asking you a question. (2) Oh, there are plenty of animal doctors. (3) I am going blind in one eye. (4) … the trouble is that anybody thinks he can doctor animals.

question_bank.stem: Underline the verbs and identify the tense form (present continuous or simple present) in sentences 1–4.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following sentences from the text: 'That man's (man has) got sense.' and 'Can't you see that it has stopped raining?' Study the present perfect tense. Fill in the blanks using the correct form of verbs (present perfect) given within brackets: Since becoming an animal doctor, Doctor Dolittle 1. _______ (learn) to communicate with all animals and birds. He 2. _______ (treat) countless farm animals and pets with care. The parrot, Polynesia, 3. _______ (teach) him bird-language, and he 4. _______ (write) books about his adventures. Many people 5. _______ (bring) their sick animals to his door. Over the years, Dolittle 6. _______ (become) renowned worldwide for his unique abilities.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks using present perfect tense: Dolittle 1._______(learn) … 2._______(treat) … Polynesia 3._______(teach) … he 4._______(write) … Many people 5._______(bring) … Dolittle 6._______(become) renowned worldwide.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: The word 'goshala' translates to 'cowshed' in English — a Sanskrit word from 'go' (cow) and 'śālā' (shed/hall). Plan a visit with the teacher to a 'goshala' and find out how the cows are looked after.

question_bank.stem: Plan a class visit to a goshala. Find out and report how the cows are looked after.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Ayurveda is the oldest existing medical system in India. Animals received good medicinal care in ancient India — Indian medical treatises like Charaka Samhita, Sushruta Samhita, and Harita Samhita contain references to the care of animals. Find out names of different organisations that work for the welfare of animals with the help of the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Research and list the names of organisations that work for the welfare of animals in India.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: What is the best way to take care of stray and abandoned animals? Consider: (1) Provide food and water for the animal; (2) Take them to an animal shelter; (3) Adopt them by taking them home. Give reasons for your responses.

question_bank.stem: Which option is the best way to take care of stray and abandoned animals — provide food and water, take to a shelter, or adopt? Give reasons.

main_sections.title: A Funny Man
main_sections.poem_text:
One day a funny kind of man
Came walking down this street
He wore a shoe upon his head
And hats upon his feet.

He raised the shoe and smiled at me,
His manners were polite;
But never had I seen before
Such a funny sounding sight.

He said, "Allow me to present
Your Highness with a rose."
And taking out a currant bun
He held it to my nose.

I staggered back against the wall
And then I answered, "Well!
I never saw a rose with such
A funny looking smell."

He then began to sing a song,
And sat down on the ground;
You never heard in all your life
Such a funny feeling sound.

"My friend, why do you wear two hats
Upon your feet?" I said.
He turned the other way about,
And hopped home on his head.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Natalie Joan's 'A Funny Man' is a light-hearted nonsense poem that derives its humour from sustained absurdist incongruity. The central figure is a man who has inverted the ordinary world — shoe on head, hats on feet — yet behaves with impeccable courtesy. The poem's tone is gleefully comic: the narrator is baffled but not hostile, calling the man 'my friend' even while questioning his attire. The recurring word 'funny' is playfully mismatched with different sense words (a 'funny sounding sight', a 'funny looking smell', a 'funny feeling sound'), creating synesthetic oxymorons that heighten the surreal atmosphere. The rhyme scheme is ABCB throughout, giving each stanza a bouncy, song-like quality suited to its comic subject. The poem ends with a final twist: when the narrator asks why the man wears hats on his feet, the man simply turns around and hops home on his head — the perfect non-answer that confirms his world is permanently upside-down. The dominant literary device is inversion, both physical and linguistic, and the poem invites readers to celebrate the absurd and find joy in breaking everyday rules of logic and language.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Look around you for things or people that make you laugh. Write them in the bubbles given below. Share the reasons for your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

listening.transcript_text: Hello. This is quite a funny incident. One day, I was so preoccupied with preparing for my presentation. After a long day of studying at a friend's place, I finally came home. Exhausted, I removed my shoes at the door and headed straight to the kitchen for some lunch. I served myself dal-chawal and ate it, still thinking about all the topics I needed to cover. Once I finished my lunch, I got up to put my shoes away in my room and placed my plate in the kitchen. However, being so preoccupied, I ended up doing the opposite! I walked to my room with my plate in hand and left it on my desk. Then, I took my shoes and placed them in the kitchen! As I stood there, confused, I realised what I had done. I couldn't help but laugh at myself. I shook my head, recovered my shoes from the kitchen and kept the plate in the kitchen.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a girl narrate a personal incident. As you listen, select the picture related to the narration.

question_bank.stem: Select the picture (from pictures 1–4) that is related to the girl's narration.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the joke given below and work in pairs, taking turns to tell it. Remember: Narrator's voice — your own voice (speak in a matter-of-fact manner); Man's voice — change to louder voice (speak with respect); God's voice — deep voice (speak in a pleasant tone). Joke: A man is talking to God. The man says, 'God, how long is a million years?' God replies, 'For me, it's about a minute.' The man asks, 'How much is a million rupees?' God smiles and replies, 'It's less than a rupee for me.' The man quickly requests, 'God, may I have a rupee?' God promptly says, 'Wait a minute.'

question_bank.stem: Work in pairs and take turns to tell the joke about the man and God, using the correct voice for each character.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, work in pairs to create a joke and tell it in front of the class.

question_bank.stem: Create your own joke in pairs and perform it in front of the class.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: A limerick is a nonsensical and funny verse consisting of five lines with rhyme scheme AABBA. Study the two limericks given. Follow the structure below and write a limerick on your own: Line 1 — Introduction of a person or place; Lines 2–4 — A silly story; Line 5 — An unexpected ending. Template: 'There was a child from _______ / Who _______ / _______ / She/He _______ / _______'

question_bank.stem: Identify the rhyme scheme of the example limericks.
Write your own limerick using the AABBA rhyme scheme and the given structure (Line 1: Introduction; Lines 2–4: Silly story; Line 5: Unexpected ending).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following phrases from the poem: walking down, taking out, sat down. These are phrasal verbs. Phrasal verbs are combinations of a verb with a preposition or adverb that together function as a single unit. Match the phrasal verbs in Column 1 with their correct meaning in Column 2: (1) take up — (i) to understand or comprehend something; (2) take after — (ii) to assume control or responsibility for something; (3) take in — (iii) to begin to study, practice, or do something; (4) take over — (iv) to become successful or popular quickly; (5) take off — (v) to resemble or look similar to (usually a family member).

question_bank.stem: Match the phrasal verbs to their meanings.
question_bank.table:
Phrasal Verb|Meaning
take up|to begin to study, practise, or do something
take after|to resemble or look similar to (usually a family member)
take in|to understand or comprehend something
take over|to assume control or responsibility for something
take off|to become successful or popular quickly

(5) Sheela _______ her father; they have the same smile.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Match the verbs in Column 1 (look, run, put, break, call) with any suitable adverbs or prepositions in Column 2 (after, away, up, for, in, on, into, down, off, out). Write their meanings in Column 3.

question_bank.stem: For each verb (look, run, put, break, call), combine with suitable prepositions/adverbs from the list and write the meaning. Example: look after — take care of.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following words from the poem: walking, staggered, hopped. These denote different ways of walking. Arrange the words given in the box (sprint, trot, jog) in increasing order of pace, fitting them into the scale: stagger → walk → 1._____ → hop → 2._____ → run → 3._____. Now, frame sentences of your own using any five phrasal verbs.

question_bank.stem: Arrange the movement words in increasing order of pace: stagger, walk, ___(1), hop, ___(2), run, ___(3). Fill blanks with: sprint, trot, jog.
Frame sentences of your own using any five phrasal verbs.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: In ancient Indian literature and mythology, there are several humorous or funny characters who play important roles — representing wit, humour, and mischief while conveying deeper moral lessons. These include Tenali Rama, Birbal, Mullah Nasruddin, and King Vikramaditya and Betal. Take any story to read and share it with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Read a story featuring one of the characters (Tenali Rama, Birbal, Mullah Nasruddin, or King Vikramaditya and Betal) and share it with the class.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: We can identify the acts of the funny man with certain modern-day activities — street performers or clowns, performance art or abstract theatre, children's entertainment and comedy shows. Sukumar Ray, an Indian poet and illustrator, wrote poems in a similar style in his book Abol Tabol. You may also share a similar poem from your native language with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Share a humorous or nonsense poem from your native language that is similar in style to 'A Funny Man'. Explain what makes it funny.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read and enjoy the poem 'My Lost Pencil' (provided on p.69). This is an additional poem for reading pleasure — no formal tasks.

main_sections.title: Say the Right Thing
main_sections.prose_summary: 'Say the Right Thing' is a humorous two-scene play by G.C. Thornley in which a mother, Mrs. Shaw, tries to teach her impulsive young daughter Mary how to behave when important guests visit. In Scene I, Mrs. Shaw prepares for the arrival of Mrs. Harding (wife of a wealthy newcomer to Lanfield) and her sister-in-law Mrs. Lee. She coaches Mary on the essentials of polite conversation: be kind, say things that please people, laugh and keep conversation flowing, and above all — when guests want to leave, say 'Must you go? Can't you stay?' rather than jumping up eagerly. Mary is confident she can manage. Scene II reveals the comic catastrophe. The moment the guests arrive, Mary puts her foot in it serially: she congratulates Mrs. Harding on her 'beautiful children', only to be told Mrs. Harding has no children. She then mocks a commuter named Mr. Best for reading newspapers instead of books and dismisses bankers as unable to think — prompting Mrs. Lee to reveal that her brother is that very banker. Mary criticises a woman who stays in bed in the mornings, and Mrs. Harding admits she does the same. She calls Mrs. Grantley 'so talkative', and Mrs. Lee quietly notes her mother is very talkative. She complains about a dirty dog outside — which turns out to be the Hardings' own dog, Towzer. She ridicules a neighbour who rides horses and practises shooting — and Mrs. Lee's brother does exactly that. Each blunder is followed by an excruciating silence. Finally, as the guests prepare to leave and Mrs. Shaw asks them to stay longer, Mary — hopelessly muddled — delivers the perfectly inverted goodbye: 'Oh, must you stay? Can't you go?' The play is a gentle but pointed comedy of social embarrassment, showing through laughing-at-oneself situations that good conversation requires listening, empathy, and thinking before speaking.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Work in pairs and discuss: (1) What does 'Athithi Devo Bhava' mean? (2) Why is it important to be polite to guests?

question_bank.stem: What does 'Athithi Devo Bhava' mean?
Why is it important to be polite to guests?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose the statements that display polite behaviour when you have guests at home: (1) Offer the guests a glass of water. (2) Enquire about the well-being of the guests. (3) Ask the guests when they would leave. (4) Comment critically about the habits of guests. (5) Accompany the guests to the door when they leave.

question_bank.stem: Which statements display polite behaviour when you have guests at home? (Choose all that apply from the list of 5.)
options: Offer the guests a glass of water.
Enquire about the well-being of the guests.
Ask the guests when they would leave.
Comment critically about the habits of guests.
Accompany the guests to the door when they leave.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Sometimes you find that a few friends in class are rude while disagreeing with each other on certain issues. Work in groups. How would you deal with such a situation? Share at least three ways in which you can do this.

question_bank.stem: How would you deal with friends who are rude while disagreeing? Share at least three ways.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (Scene I). Complete the table based on Scene I of the play. Speaker | Words of the Speaker | Reason. Example row given (Mrs. Shaw / 'The room looks cleaner now. Put that coat away in your bedroom.' / To instruct Mary to keep the room tidy). Complete the remaining rows for Mary and Mrs. Shaw.

question_bank.stem: Complete the table: Speaker | Words of the Speaker | Reason. Fill in the missing entries for Mary and Mrs. Shaw.
question_bank.table:
Speaker|Words of the Speaker|Reason
Mrs. Shaw|The room looks cleaner now. Put that coat away in your bedroom.|To instruct Mary to keep the room tidy (given)
Mary|You're always telling me to put things away.|
Mary|I'm not a child!|
Mrs. Shaw||To highlight the importance of being kind as a way to ensure Mary says the appropriate things when guests arrive

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think Mary will be able to follow her mother's advice successfully when Mrs. Harding and Mrs. Lee visit? Why or why not?

question_bank.stem: Do you think Mary will be able to follow her mother's advice successfully when Mrs. Harding and Mrs. Lee visit? Why or why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us discuss (Scene II). Rectify the false statements given below: (1) Mrs. Harding states that they do not like London as it is not the same as Lanfield. (2) Mrs. Harding was pleased to know that Mary was familiar with her children. (3) Mrs. Shaw shared that she liked Mrs. Best as she read a lot of newspapers. (4) Mrs. Harding and Mrs. Shaw agree that wearing a blue dress with a red coat is unfashionable. (5) Mary shares that she finds the dog outside the window cute.

question_bank.stem: Rectify the false statement: Mrs. Harding states that they do not like London as it is not the same as Lanfield.
Rectify the false statement: Mrs. Harding was pleased to know that Mary was familiar with her children.
Rectify the false statement: Mrs. Shaw shared that she liked Mrs. Best as she read a lot of newspapers.
Rectify the false statement: Mrs. Harding and Mrs. Shaw agree that wearing a blue dress with a red coat is unfashionable.
Rectify the false statement: Mary shares that she finds the dog outside the window cute.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us think and reflect. Read extract 1 (Mary and Mrs. Shaw exchange about talking) and answer the questions.

question_bank.stem: What does Mrs. Shaw imply when she says, 'One day you'll have to go to some of these houses yourself and talk to the people there'?
options: She believes Mary should start learning about hosting guests then.
She thinks Mary is ready to visit these houses independently.
She wants Mary to develop social skills for the future.
She hopes Mary will avoid these social interactions entirely.

question_bank.stem: Mrs. Shaw and Mary are discussing how to ______ (have a polite conversation / get to know people better).
Why did Mary insist on staying with her mother when the guests arrived?
Match the following character traits to the characters: A. eager, B. inexperienced, C. cautious — Mary / Mrs. Shaw.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read extract 2 (Mary's comments about Mrs. Cotter, and Mrs. Harding's response) and answer the questions.

question_bank.stem: Mary could spot Mrs. Cotter in the street because ______.
What does Mary's comment about Mrs. Cotter staying in bed and calling the doctor suggest about her understanding?
options: She is worried about why Mrs. Cotter stays in bed all morning.
She is unable to understand why people need medical help.
She thinks Mrs. Cotter is scared and lonely for staying in bed.
She believes Mrs. Cotter is right to rest and take care of herself.

question_bank.stem: How can we say that Mary enjoyed good health?
Identify whether the following statement is true or false: Mary might have unintentionally offended Mrs. Harding.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions: (1) What does Mary's comment about Mr. Best's routine and profession reveal about her viewpoint on bankers and their way of life? (2) What can we infer about Mary's understanding of polite conversation based on her interactions with the guests? (3) What do you think the repeated stage direction 'no one speaks' indicates about the atmosphere in the room when Mary speaks? (4) How does Mary's attitude towards the guests change throughout the play? (5) What do Mrs. Harding's and Mrs. Lee's responses to Mary's blunders during their visit reveal about their characters? (6) In what way does the play show the importance of knowing how to engage in polite conversation?

question_bank.stem: What does Mary's comment about Mr. Best's routine and profession reveal about her viewpoint on bankers and their way of life?
What can we infer about Mary's understanding of polite conversation based on her interactions with the guests?
What do you think the repeated stage direction 'no one speaks' indicates about the atmosphere in the room when Mary speaks?
How does Mary's attitude towards the guests change throughout the play?
What do Mrs. Harding's and Mrs. Lee's responses to Mary's blunders reveal about their characters?
In what way does the play show the importance of knowing how to engage in polite conversation?

listening.transcript_text: Mother: Why were you getting impatient in the grocery store? Son: Was I really? I just didn't like waiting so long. Mother: I know waiting can be boring, but remember, we always stand in queues and wait for our turn. It's fair for everyone, right? Son: Hmm, I guess. Even if it takes a lot of time? Mother: Well, that's when we practise patience. And when it's our turn, what do we say to the shopkeeper? Son: We say, 'Thank you'. And 'Please' if we ask for something, right? Mother: Yes! Those are polite words. Why do you think they're important? Son: Maybe because they make people feel respected? Mother: Exactly! It shows gratitude and kindness. And what about yesterday when you bumped into that lady with the blue sari? Son: Oh, I said sorry right away. You always tell me it's important to apologise. Mother: That's true. Why do you think it's important to apologise? Son: Um… so people know I didn't mean to do it and that I care? Mother: Yes, it shows you're responsible. And how about when we're in places like the library? What kind of voice should we use? Son: Oh, I know this! We speak softly so we don't bother others. Mother: Right again! It's called using an 'inside voice'. Son: I know. I also know that we should cover our mouth when we yawn, sneeze or cough in a gathering or a public place. Mother: Absolutely! You're getting really good at remembering these things. And when we talk at home or school, what should we do when someone else is speaking? Son: We wait for our turn. It's not well-mannered to interrupt. Mother: You've got it, Rohan. You're becoming a really polite young man. Son: Thanks, Ma. So, do you think I'll be ready to handle the grocery queue next time? Mother: Certainly! And maybe we can get some ice cream as a reward if you do it well. Son: Deal! And… thank you!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a mother and her son about basic manners. As you listen, fill in the blanks given below by selecting the correct option.

question_bank.stem: How did the son behave in the grocery queue? (i) strangely (ii) impatiently (iii) politely
The mother finally refers to her son as a ______. (i) polite young man (ii) responsible son (iii) grateful person

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will once again listen to the conversation. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below: (1) The mother uses the grocery store incident as an opportunity to teach her son about patience and fairness. (2) The son recognises the value of apologising and understands that it shows he notices others. (3) The son is gradually learning the importance of polite words and actions from his mother's guidance. (4) The mother discourages her son from speaking softly in public, suggesting that people should adjust to his voice level. (5) The son feels that covering his mouth when coughing is only important when he's at home. (6) The son receives all advice readily while understanding its importance. (7) The mother uses praise to support the polite behaviours she wants her son to continue practising.

question_bank.stem: Select four TRUE statements from the list of seven about the mother-son conversation on manners.
options: 1 - Mother uses grocery incident to teach patience and fairness.
3 - Son gradually learns importance of polite words from mother's guidance.
4 - Mother discourages son from speaking softly in public.
5 - Son feels covering mouth when coughing is only for home.
6 - Son receives all advice readily while understanding its importance.
7 - Mother uses praise to support polite behaviours.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: As you know, intonation is the rise and fall of pitch of tone while speaking. Sentence stress is laying emphasis on certain word(s) in a sentence. Speak the given declarative sentence aloud: 'I saw Mrs. Cotter this afternoon.' Use falling intonation towards end of the sentence. Stress the words 'Mrs. Cotter'. Then speak the following declarative sentences with falling intonation and stress the highlighted words: (1) The room looks cleaner now. (2) You always talk too much. (3) You leave your clothes all over the house.

question_bank.stem: Speak the sentence 'I saw Mrs. Cotter this afternoon' with falling intonation, stressing 'Mrs. Cotter'.
Speak with falling intonation and stress the highlighted words: (1) The room looks cleaner now. (2) You always talk too much. (3) You leave your clothes all over the house.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs and speak sentences 1–5 aloud as Mary, applying stress and falling intonation. Match each to its meaning (i)–(v): 1. I didn't hurt anyone today. 2. I didn't hurt anyone today. 3. I didn't hurt anyone today. 4. I didn't hurt anyone today. 5. I didn't hurt anyone today. Meanings: (i) I might have done other things but did not hurt anyone. (ii) I might cause hurt usually, but not anyone on this day. (iii) The action of hurting anyone did not occur. (iv) I, specifically, did not hurt anyone, possibly implying someone else might have. (v) I may have spoken many things but not a single person was hurt.

question_bank.stem: Match each stress pattern of 'I didn't hurt anyone today' (sentences 1–5, each stressing a different word) to its correct meaning (i)–(v).
question_bank.table:
Sentence (stressed word)|Meaning
I (stressed)|(iv) I, specifically, did not hurt anyone
didn't (stressed)|(iii) The action of hurting anyone did not occur
hurt (stressed)|(i) I might have done other things but did not hurt anyone
anyone (stressed)|(v) I may have spoken many things but not a single person was hurt
today (stressed)|(ii) I might cause hurt usually, but not on this day

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, speak the given declarative sentence with rising intonation towards the end: 'I saw Mrs. Cotter this afternoon.' Understand that the rising intonation makes it sound like a question — Did I see Mrs. Cotter this afternoon?

question_bank.stem: Speak 'I saw Mrs. Cotter this afternoon' with rising intonation and explain how it changes the meaning.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Rewrite the play 'Say the Right Thing' as a short story, focusing on Mary's interactions with her mother and the guests. Ensure the story captures the main events, character emotions, and humorous moments effectively. Use paragraphs to organise your story and include descriptive language. Hints: Begin with introduction of Mary and Mrs. Shaw; explain Mrs. Shaw's instructions and Mary's confidence; narrate events when guests visit; include key moments of humour and awkwardness; conclude by commenting on how the visit ended.

question_bank.stem: Rewrite the play 'Say the Right Thing' as a short story (following the hints given), capturing Mary's blunders, the guests' reactions, and the humorous ending.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the following words: humorous, hilariously, coming, beautiful. These are made by adding suffixes -ous, -ly, -ing, -ful to root words. Now divide the following words from the text as root words + suffixes: gladly, banker, guidance, quietly, walking.

question_bank.stem: Divide each word into root word + suffix: gladly, banker, guidance, quietly, walking.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the words in Column 1. Match the suffixes in Column 2 with their meanings in Column 3. Write examples in Column 4. Column 1: Biology, tolerance, stopped, backward, courageous, quietly, toughest, tomatoes. Column 2: (i)-logy, (ii)-ance, (iii)-ed, (iv)-ward, (v)-ous, (vi)-ly, (vii)-est, (viii)-es. Column 3: A.change to adverb, B.change to plural, C.a study of, D.change to superlative, E.change to past tense, F.change to a noun, G.in a certain direction, H.change to adjective.

question_bank.stem: Match suffix in Column 2 with its meaning in Column 3 and write examples in Column 4.
question_bank.table:
Word|Suffix|Meaning|Examples
Biology|-logy|a study of|Sociology (given)
tolerance|-ance|change to a noun|
stopped|-ed|change to past tense|
backward|-ward|in a certain direction|
courageous|-ous|change to adjective|
quietly|-ly|change to adverb|
toughest|-est|change to superlative|
tomatoes|-es|change to plural|

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Fill in the blanks using words given in the brackets by adding suitable suffixes: To communicate (i)_______(effective), choose your words and topics (ii)_______(wise). Avoid (iii)_______(discuss) things you don't know much about. Remember to be (iv)_______(respect) towards others, and express your (v)_______(appreciate). Finally, always be (vi)_______(response) by replying promptly.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks by adding suitable suffixes to the words in brackets: (i)_______(effective) (ii)_______(wise) (iii)_______(discuss) (iv)_______(respect) (v)_______(appreciate) (vi)_______(response).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the highlighted words: 'But does anyone want to hear the things that you say?' and 'I can't tell you everything.' 'Say' focuses on the message and does not always require a listener. 'Tell' focuses on who receives the message and always needs a listener. Fill in the blanks with suitable words from the sets: fetch/bring, see/look, live/stay, study/learn, speak/talk, hear/listen.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks: (i) I _______ at the photo, but I didn't _______ anyone familiar. (ii) I'd better go and _______ my mother from the station, and then _______ her home. (iii) I need to _______ for my mathematics exam this weekend, so I hope to _______ new concepts. (iv) I can _______ the music from my neighbour's house, but I need to _______ to it carefully. (v) 'Could I _______ to Mrs. Kalpana, who is going to _______ to us about gardening.' (vi) I _______ in a small town, but I often _______ with my relatives in the city.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read sentences from the text: 'They lived in London before.' and 'I met her in a shop and asked her to come.' (simple past). Also: 'She has been staying with them for a while.' (present perfect continuous). We use present perfect continuous (has/have + been + verb + -ing) to indicate duration of action started in the past that continues to present, or ongoing recent action with results evident now. Fill in the blanks using verbs in present continuous or present perfect continuous tense: The students (i)_______(organise) a play and setting up the stage. They (ii)_______(prepare) for weeks to make everything perfect. The performers (iii)_______(rehearse) every day, and their excitement (iv)_______(grow). The sound in-charge (v)_______(test) the microphones. Everyone (vi)_______(work) hard, and now the place (vii)_______(buzz) with energy.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blanks using present continuous or present perfect continuous tense: (i)_______(organise) (ii)_______(prepare) (iii)_______(rehearse) (iv)_______(grow) (v)_______(test) (vi)_______(work) (vii)_______(buzz).

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the explanation about kinds of sentences: Declarative (information, full stop), Interrogative (question, question mark), Exclamatory (strong feelings, exclamation mark), Imperative (order/command, full stop). Identify the kinds of sentences and punctuate them: (i) the meeting starts at 10.00 a.m. in the conference room (ii) what an exciting adventure it was (iii) how did you solve the problem so quickly (iv) what is the weather forecast today (v) how beautiful is the rainbow (vi) clean up your room before dinner (vii) the children are planning a surprise party for their mom (viii) turn off the music.

question_bank.stem: Identify the kind of sentence and add correct punctuation for each: (i) the meeting starts at 10.00 a.m. in the conference room (ii) what an exciting adventure it was (iii) how did you solve the problem so quickly (iv) what is the weather forecast today (v) how beautiful is the rainbow (vi) clean up your room before dinner (vii) the children are planning a surprise party for their mom (viii) turn off the music.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in groups. Enact your favourite part of the play. Remember to: allot a specific role to each member in the group (director, narrator, and characters); include the actions and expressions as indicated in brackets in the play.

question_bank.stem: Enact your favourite part of 'Say the Right Thing' in groups, assigning roles: director, narrator, and characters. Include stage directions.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Find out how people greet guests who visit their homes in different languages. Share it with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Research and share how people in different cultures and languages greet guests at home.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: 'Vasudhaiva Kuṭumbakaṁ' is a Sanskrit phrase which means 'the world is one family'. It originates from ancient Indian texts like the Maha Upanishad and the Hitopadesha. It was chosen as the theme for the G20 Summit. Discuss how this phrase connects to the theme of polite and kind interaction with guests seen in the play.

question_bank.stem: How does 'Vasudhaiva Kuṭumbakaṁ' (the world is one family) connect to the theme of polite and respectful interaction with guests as shown in the play?
